title: Writing Your First Test
description: Create your first Playwright test from scratch, understanding test structure, basic commands, and assertions.
objectives: Understand the anatomy of a Playwright test
Write basic test cases with navigation and interactions
Use assertions to verify expected outcomes
Run and debug your first test

sections.title: Test Structure Basics
sections.content:
# Understanding Test Structure

A Playwright test consists of several key components:

## Import Statement

Every test file starts by importing the test runner and assertion library:

```typescript
import { test, expect } from '@playwright/test';
```

- `test`: Function to define test cases and hooks
- `expect`: Assertion library for verifying conditions

## Test Block

Tests are defined using the `test()` function:

```typescript
test('test name', async ({ page }) => {
  // Test code here
});
```

**Components**:
- **Test name**: Descriptive string explaining what the test does
- **async**: Tests are asynchronous (they perform actions that take time)
- **{ page }**: Fixtures provided by Playwright (we'll cover more fixtures later)
- **Test body**: The actual test code

## The Page Fixture

The `page` fixture is your main interface to the browser:
- Represents a single browser tab/page
- Automatically created and cleaned up for each test
- Provides methods for navigation, interaction, and assertions

## Test Lifecycle

Each test follows this lifecycle:
1. **Setup**: Browser context and page are created
2. **Execution**: Your test code runs
3. **Teardown**: Browser context and page are closed automatically

No manual cleanup needed - Playwright handles it!

sections.title: Your First Test
sections.explanation: This is a complete, working test. It navigates to a website and verifies content. Notice how simple and readable it is - this is the power of Playwright's API.

sections.title: Basic Navigation
Basic Interactions
sections.explanation: This example shows common user interactions. Playwright makes them simple with intuitive method names and automatic waiting.

sections.title: Assertions
sections.explanation: Playwright assertions are powerful and auto-wait. They keep retrying until the condition is met or timeout occurs, making tests reliable.

sections.title: Complete Test Example
sections.explanation: This is a complete test suite testing a todo application. Notice how we group related tests, verify each action, and test complete user workflows.

sections.title: Running and Debugging
sections.content:
# Running Your Tests

## Command Line

```bash
# Run all tests
npx playwright test

# Run a specific file
npx playwright test first-test.spec.ts

# Run tests in headed mode (see browser)
npx playwright test --headed

# Run in specific browser
npx playwright test --project=chromium

# Run with UI mode (interactive)
npx playwright test --ui
```

## Debugging Tests

### Method 1: Debug Mode

```bash
npx playwright test --debug
```

This opens Playwright Inspector where you can:
- Step through test line by line
- See page state at each step
- Explore locators
- Time-travel through test execution

### Method 2: VS Code Debugger

1. Set a breakpoint by clicking left of line number
2. Click "Debug Test" button in VS Code
3. Use VS Code's debug controls to step through

### Method 3: Using console.log()

```typescript
test('debugging example', async ({ page }) => {
  await page.goto('https://example.com');
  
  // Log to see what's happening
  console.log(await page.title());
  
  const button = page.locator('button');
  console.log(await button.textContent());
  console.log(await button.isVisible());
});
```

### Method 4: Pause Test Execution

```typescript
test('pause example', async ({ page }) => {
  await page.goto('https://example.com');
  
  // Pause and inspect
  await page.pause();
  
  // Test continues when you click Resume
  await page.click('button');
});
```

## Understanding Test Output

```
Running 3 tests using 3 workers

  ✓ first-test.spec.ts:3:1 › visit Playwright homepage (1s)
  ✓ interactions.spec.ts:3:1 › basic interactions (2s)
  ✓ todo-app.spec.ts:5:3 › Todo Application › user can add todos (1s)

  3 passed (4s)
```

**Understanding the output**:
- ✓ indicates passed test
- Time in parentheses is execution time
- Tests run in parallel by default
- Total time is less than sum of individual tests

## Common Errors

### TimeoutError
```
TimeoutError: page.click: Timeout 30000ms exceeded.
```
**Cause**: Element not found or not clickable
**Solution**: Check selector, increase timeout, or verify element exists

### Navigation Error
```
Error: page.goto: net::ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED
```
**Cause**: Server not running or wrong URL
**Solution**: Verify URL, start development server

### Assertion Error
```
Error: expect(locator).toHaveText(expected)
Expected: "Welcome"
Received: "Welcome Back"
```
**Cause**: Actual text doesn't match expected
**Solution**: Update expectation or fix application

sections.title: Best Practices

keyTakeaways: Playwright tests are async functions that receive fixtures like 'page'
Always use 'await' with Playwright methods for navigation and interactions
Assertions with expect() automatically retry until conditions are met
Use test.describe() to group related tests logically
Tests should follow the AAA pattern: Arrange, Act, Assert
Playwright provides excellent debugging tools including --debug and page.pause()
Keep tests independent - each test should set up its own state
Use descriptive test names that explain what is being tested
